terms.definition: Imaging modality using X-rays to produce two-dimensional projection images of anatomical structures.
terms.exampleSentence: Plain radiography of the chest is the first-line investigation for suspected pneumonia.
terms.contextNotes: Often shortened to 'X-ray' in patient communication.

terms.definition: Cross-sectional imaging modality using rotating X-ray beams and detectors to reconstruct tomographic slices.
terms.exampleSentence: Computed tomography of the head was performed to exclude an acute intracranial bleed.
terms.contextNotes: Higher radiation dose than plain radiography.

terms.definition: Cross-sectional imaging modality using strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses; no ionising radiation.
terms.exampleSentence: Magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine demonstrated a left L5/S1 disc protrusion.
terms.contextNotes: Strict safety screening required for ferromagnetic implants.

terms.definition: Imaging modality using high-frequency sound waves and a transducer to produce real-time images of soft tissues.
terms.exampleSentence: An abdominal ultrasound was requested to assess the gallbladder for stones.
terms.contextNotes: Operator-dependent; no ionising radiation.

terms.definition: Real-time X-ray imaging used to visualise dynamic processes such as contrast studies and interventional procedures.
terms.exampleSentence: Fluoroscopy was used to guide insertion of the central venous catheter.
terms.contextNotes: Cumulative dose tracked via fluoroscopy time.

terms.definition: Low-dose X-ray imaging of the breast used for screening and diagnostic evaluation of breast tissue.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was referred for bilateral mammography following a palpable lump.
terms.contextNotes: Compression improves image quality and reduces dose.

terms.definition: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry used to measure bone mineral density for osteoporosis assessment.
terms.exampleSentence: A DEXA scan revealed a T-score of -2.6, consistent with osteoporosis.
terms.contextNotes: Very low radiation dose; lumbar spine and hip standard sites.

terms.definition: Positron emission tomography; functional imaging using radiotracers to map metabolic activity, often combined with CT.
terms.exampleSentence: A PET-CT was arranged to stage the patient's newly diagnosed lung cancer.
terms.contextNotes: Patient must fast and limit activity prior to FDG injection.

terms.definition: Single-photon emission computed tomography; nuclear medicine imaging producing tomographic functional images.
terms.exampleSentence: A myocardial perfusion SPECT was requested to evaluate suspected coronary ischaemia.
terms.contextNotes: Lower spatial resolution than PET but more widely available.

terms.definition: Imaging speciality using unsealed radiopharmaceuticals to assess organ function and disease processes.
terms.exampleSentence: Nuclear medicine performed a bone scan to investigate possible metastatic disease.
terms.contextNotes: Patients are mildly radioactive after injection; advise on contact precautions.

terms.definition: Imaging of blood vessels using intravascular contrast, performed via catheter, CT or MR techniques.
terms.exampleSentence: CT angiography of the pulmonary arteries excluded a pulmonary embolism.
terms.contextNotes: Check renal function and contrast allergy before iodinated studies.

terms.definition: Image-guided minimally invasive procedures performed by radiographers and radiologists for diagnosis and treatment.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was referred to interventional radiography for percutaneous drainage of a hepatic abscess.
terms.contextNotes: Requires informed consent and pre-procedure coagulation check.

terms.definition: Anteroposterior projection; X-ray beam enters anteriorly and exits posteriorly, commonly used in supine patients.
terms.exampleSentence: An AP chest radiograph was acquired at the bedside as the patient could not stand.
terms.contextNotes: Cardiac silhouette appears magnified compared with PA view.

terms.definition: Posteroanterior projection; X-ray beam enters posteriorly and exits anteriorly, standard for erect chest radiography.
terms.exampleSentence: A PA chest radiograph is preferred for accurate cardiothoracic ratio assessment.
terms.contextNotes: Patient stands facing the detector with hands on hips.

terms.definition: Side-on projection in which the X-ray beam passes through the body from one side to the other.
terms.exampleSentence: A lateral chest radiograph helps localise lesions seen on the PA view.
terms.contextNotes: Always specify left or right lateral on the request.

terms.definition: Angled projection between AP/PA and lateral, used to demonstrate structures free of superimposition.
terms.exampleSentence: Oblique views of the cervical spine were obtained to assess the intervertebral foramina.
terms.contextNotes: Specify degree of rotation and side rotated towards the detector.

terms.definition: Patient position lying flat on the back with face upward; used when erect imaging is not possible.
terms.exampleSentence: The trauma patient remained supine on the trolley during imaging.
terms.contextNotes: Air-fluid levels may be missed on supine films.

terms.definition: Patient position lying face down on the abdomen, with the back facing upward.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was placed prone for the CT-guided lung biopsy.
terms.contextNotes: Check airway and monitoring before turning prone.

terms.definition: Patient lying on their side with a horizontal X-ray beam; useful for detecting free air or fluid levels.
terms.exampleSentence: A left lateral decubitus abdominal film was requested to look for free intraperitoneal air.
terms.contextNotes: Side specified is the side the patient is lying on.

terms.definition: Patient position standing or sitting upright; preferred for chest and abdominal imaging when feasible.
terms.exampleSentence: An erect PA chest radiograph was performed to assess for pneumoperitoneum.
terms.contextNotes: Allows free gas to rise and fluid levels to settle.
commonMistakes: forgetting to mark erect on film

terms.definition: General term for a patient lying down, including supine, prone or lateral positions.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was recumbent throughout the lengthy MRI examination.
terms.contextNotes: Specify type of recumbency on the request.
commonMistakes: using as synonym only for supine

terms.definition: Right anterior oblique; patient rotated so the right anterior chest wall is closest to the detector.
terms.exampleSentence: An RAO view was acquired to demonstrate the right ventricular outflow tract.
terms.contextNotes: Naming based on the side closest to the image receptor.

terms.definition: Left anterior oblique; patient rotated so the left anterior chest wall is closest to the detector.
terms.exampleSentence: The cardiologist requested an LAO projection during the angiogram.
terms.contextNotes: Standard view in cardiac fluoroscopy and angiography.

terms.definition: Right posterior oblique; patient rotated so the right posterior chest wall is closest to the detector.
terms.exampleSentence: An RPO projection of the lumbar spine was obtained to assess the right pars interarticularis.
terms.contextNotes: Mirror image of LAO when imaging supine patients.

terms.definition: Left posterior oblique; patient rotated so the left posterior chest wall is closest to the detector.
terms.exampleSentence: The LPO view demonstrated the left sacroiliac joint in profile.
terms.contextNotes: Mirror image of RAO when patient is supine.

terms.definition: Imaging technique acquiring multiple low-dose projections to reconstruct thin slices, commonly used in breast imaging.
terms.exampleSentence: Digital breast tomosynthesis improved detection of architectural distortion in dense breasts.
terms.contextNotes: Reduces tissue overlap compared with conventional mammography.

terms.definition: Bedside imaging using a portable X-ray unit for patients unable to attend the imaging department.
terms.exampleSentence: Mobile radiography was used to confirm the position of the endotracheal tube on ITU.
terms.contextNotes: Maintain a 2-metre safety distance and warn surrounding staff.

terms.definition: Device that captures the X-ray beam after attenuation by the patient and converts it into an image signal.
terms.exampleSentence: The image receptor was placed behind the patient's chest for the PA view.
terms.contextNotes: Modern systems use flat-panel digital detectors.

terms.definition: Anatomical landmark at which the central X-ray beam is directed to ensure correct projection geometry.
terms.exampleSentence: The centring point for a PA chest is at the level of T7 in the midline.
terms.contextNotes: Incorrect centring causes anatomical cut-off and repeats.

terms.definition: Restriction of the X-ray beam to the area of clinical interest, reducing patient dose and improving image quality.
terms.exampleSentence: Tight collimation to the elbow joint reduced unnecessary exposure to surrounding tissues.
terms.contextNotes: Required by IR(ME)R as part of dose optimisation.
